chapters.title: History explains who we are now
chapters.text:
Reality's timeline stretches from physics — matter and energy emerging 13.5 billion years ago — through chemistry's atoms and molecules, biology's first organisms 3.8 billion years back, to Homo sapiens roughly 70,000 years ago.

Three revolutions redefined human existence:

• The Cognitive Revolution (~70,000 years ago) expanded imagination and shared belief.
• The Agricultural Revolution (~12,000 years ago) shifted humanity from foraging to farming.
• The Scientific Revolution (~500 years ago) transformed inquiry through math, physics, astronomy, biology, and chemistry.

Our neural sophistication made sapiens Earth's dominant species. Extended childhood required communal care; social skill became survival skill. Brains still crave kinship networks as lifelines.

Tracing that arc clarifies modern behavior the way childhood explains personality. To glimpse the future, decode the past.

We must unravel the central insights of our past to know who we are and what our future holds.

Stay tuned to learn how humanity has evolved and transformed over time. Just as our childhood development has shaped who we are and why we act the way we do, history molds our behavior and thoughts.

It all began 13.5 billion years ago with the emergence of matter and energy, marking the birth of physics. Roughly 300,000 years later, matter and energy evolved into atoms and molecules, revealing the secrets of chemistry.

Biology first appeared 3.8 billion years ago with the emergence of the first organisms, while the evolution of Homo sapiens 70,000 years ago set the stage for human history. Throughout the years, three monumental revolutions shaped humanity: • The Cognitive Revolution, commencing around 70,000 years ago, ushered in the advent of the human mind.

chapters.title: Cognitive Revolution: fiction as social glue
chapters.text:
Multiple human species once coexisted until Homo sapiens prevailed. Debate continues — interbreeding versus replacement — but language and shared myth distinguished our line.

Ants communicate for tasks; they do not gossip about relationships. Apes approach our social complexity, yet only sapiens spin nations, gods, and corporations into collective reality.

The Cognitive Revolution weaponized imagination. People die for countries, labor inside legal systems, and worship in temples built for entities that exist because millions believe together. Money, rights, and brands are intersubjective inventions — powerful because belief is contagious.

DNA still carries hunter-gatherer appetites. High-calorie cravings made sense on the savanna; stocked refrigerators cannot silence ancient urges.

Relationship norms varied widely across cultures — monogamy, polygamy, heterosexual, homosexual — without a single "natural" template.

There is no correct or "natural" way to live but a vast array of cultural choices to explore and embrace.

Foragers mastered local ecology, worked modest hours, and gossiped strategically to track tribal alliances. Animism placed souls in animals, plants, and phenomena — denying human supremacy over nature.

In the past, various human species coexisted until Homo sapiens conquered the world and replaced them. Scientists are still searching for a conclusive answer regarding how the transition happened.

One theory proposes interbreeding, while another proposes the replacement theory, suggesting that Homo sapiens killed rival species. How did we evolve from primitive creatures into beings who rule the planet?

One of the main reasons is our unique and flexible language, which can express the intricate details of our complex world. For example, while ants are adept at communication and collaboration for simple tasks, they cannot delve into family matters, emotions, or social hierarchies.

In contrast, we share similarities in these aspects with apes. Still, our ability to create complex myths, gods, and other works of fiction grants us supremacy over the world and unites us as a species.

chapters.title: Agriculture's gains and hidden costs
chapters.text:
Twelve thousand years ago farming ignited population growth, cities, and complex institutions. Progress carried a price.

A child's death from failed crops illustrates vulnerability introduced by sedentary life. Disease spread; diets narrowed; labor intensified while well-being often stagnated.

The dream of an easier existence frequently delivered more obligations — mortgages, dependents, and endless acquisition. Luxury masqueraded as simplicity while inner emptiness persisted.

Digital connectivity promised relief yet delivered notification anxiety. Genuine advancement should enlarge fulfillment, not merely workload.

We must ensure that we live a fulfilling life and that luxury doesn't enslave us.

Animals paid heavily too: mutilated pigs, separated calves, cage-bound livestock raised for tender meat. Progress funded by suffering demands ethical reckoning — and better choices remain possible.

The Agricultural Revolution began around 12,000 years ago and gave rise to farming, a pivotal step in our evolution. This breakthrough paved the way for population growth and the formation of more complex social structures, such as cities and states, allowing political, economic, and cultural institutions to emerge.

Even though the revolution’s upsides are undeniable, we should also be aware of the drawbacks and the price we paid for the progress. One cost of our evolution is the death of a four-year-old boy because there was too much rain, and his father's rice didn't grow.

Settling in one place and working harder contributed to the spreading of illnesses and diseases; relying on a limited range of crops led to a less diverse and nutritious diet. Despite toiling harder than their ancestors, people found themselves trapped in a lousy quality of life with no way to turn.

Then, the desire for an easier life became a double-edged sword, trapping us with even more duties and struggles. Since the Agricultural Revolution, we have misinterpreted luxury for simplicity.

Many dedicate most of their time to work, hoping to one day have enough time for their true passions. Yet this desire is accompanied by a mortgage, kids, and the drive to possess more.

chapters.title: Hierarchy as invented order
chapters.text:
Modern societies preach equality, yet history normalized rank — white over Black and Indigenous people, men over women, rich over poor — without biological justification.

Discrimination hardened through arbitrary events and mythic reinforcement. Slavery's end did not erase segregation when stereotypes outlived law.

People created religious and scientific reasons for inequality, mostly to justify slavery, obtain power, and feel superior.

Gender gaps stem more from culture than anatomy. Patriarchy after agriculture trained feminine and masculine scripts. Competing theories — strength, aggression, reproductive strategy — each leave gaps; matriarchal species show alternative outcomes.

Women can bear children, and while childrearing is a natural ability, it's not their only purpose. Moreover, the labels "natural" and "unnatural" come from Christian theology. Biology says unnatural things don't exist, and all possibilities are natural.

Nowadays, equality is our fundamental value. Yet before that, people believed hierarchy was the key to maintaining order, prosperity, and stability.

Despite efforts to promote equality, such as the American Declaration of Independence, the supremacy of certain groups over marginalized ones still reigned: white people over Black people and Indians, men over women, and rich over poor. These “fictions” emerged from the human mind, as no scientific evidence exists to support that white people and Black people have any biological differences beyond physical appearance.

Discrimination arose alongside arbitrary historical events, perpetuating the established social order for future generations. Even after the abolition of slavery, segregation persisted due to myths and stereotypes around Black people and how society treated them for centuries.

Even though men and women are slightly different biologically, gender inequality is the product of social and cultural constructs rather than biological differences. Since the Agricultural Revolution, most societies have been patriarchal, raising and teaching women to lead a feminine life and men, a masculine one.

chapters.title: Money, empire, and faith unite strangers
chapters.text:
Money translates trust into exchange, enabling vast cooperation — and deepening inequality when wealth eclipses other values.

Empires spread ideas, goods, and institutions alongside violence and ruin.

Religions supply belonging and moral frameworks. Buddhism, founded by Siddhartha Gautama, diagnoses suffering as craving — pleasant experiences clung to, unpleasant ones resisted.

When the mind experiences something distasteful, it craves to be rid of the irritation. When the mind experiences something pleasant, it craves that the pleasure will remain and will intensify. ~ Yuval Noah Harari

Observing sensation without grasping leads toward nirvana — release from craving and constructed self.

Humanity progressed through animism, polytheism, and monotheism toward scrutinized, flexible belief. The Scientific Revolution in sixteenth-century Europe embraced ignorance as fuel: admitting unknowns accelerates discovery.

The ability to question our beliefs and admit our ignorance accelerates our progress.

Money, a psychological construct representing imagined value, emerged in response to the need to exchange goods and favors. It fostered massive cooperation, allowing us to transform land into food and convert health into knowledge.

With money, we have built robust economies but perpetuated inequality and switched our focus to wealth at the expense of other values. Empires have brought along cruelty, violence, destruction, and war, leaving behind a trail of suffering.

Yet they have also played a significant role in spreading ideas, goods, institutions, technology, and cultures. Religion has shaped human values and beliefs, made us feel connected, and given us a sense of belonging.

Buddhism, one of the major religions, is centered around Siddhartha Gautama, a human who questioned why people experience unhappiness, anxiety, and frustration regardless of their possessions or actions. He concluded that suffering arises from cognitive patterns of behavior.

chapters.title: Science, capital, and their moral debts
chapters.text:
Five centuries of science and technology reshaped civilization. Where religion claimed completeness, empiricism pursued evidence — often steered by power's priorities.

Captain James Cook's Pacific voyages advanced knowledge while enabling colonization; Tasmanian extinction blurred exploration with conquest. Science and imperialism advanced hand in hand.

Capitalism — private ownership chasing profit — colonized minds as well as markets, financing expeditions that returned wealth through slavery and extraction.

Without ethical values — capitalism lacks justice.

The system drives innovation yet breeds pollution, exploitation, and consumerism. Responses include learning from historical error, recognizing communism's own failures, and hoping economic growth eventually lifts need — while actively addressing harm today.

Did you know? Roughly ten million enslaved Africans crossed the Atlantic between the sixteenth and nineteenth centuries under expanding free-trade economies.

In the last five centuries, science and technology have enabled humans to advance beyond expectations. While religion believes that it holds all the answers around life, science uses empirical research to find solutions.

Once we realized that disasters and global problems occur because of our ignorance, our focus shifted toward scientific inquiry. Scientists have conducted research and studies in fields aligned with our religious, economic, and political interests or the wishes of those in power.

For example, Captain James Cook's voyages helped to pave the way for the later colonization of new regions. His expedition to Tasmania resulted in the tragic violence and extinction of the Tasmanian people.

Was it a scientific expedition or a military operation to spread European imperialism? There has always been a connection between science and imperialism, as conquering required scientific developments that helped them expand.

chapters.title: Industry, individuality, and the happiness puzzle
chapters.text:
Two centuries ago steam power launched mass production and consumer culture. Advertising convinces us products repair inadequacy we never possessed.

Escape the confines of materialistic fulfillment to embrace the power of enriching experiences, meaningful connections, and personal growth.

Industrial society shifted elder care from family to markets offering jobs, pensions, and insurance. Freedom to choose partners and careers brought isolation — communal instincts clash with modern atomization. Corporations, nations, religions, and consumer tribes replace village belonging.

Progress cut famine and violence for many while creating environmental crisis and fresh misery. Forager brains inhabit capitalist cities; animals became production units.

Happiness does not really depend on objective conditions of either wealth, health or even community. Rather, it depends on the correlation between objective conditions and subjective expectations. ~ Yuval Noah Harari

Expectations — biochemical and narrative — may matter more than GDP. Meaning can be constructed even when science denies cosmic purpose.

Approximately 200 years ago, the beginning of the Industrial Revolution marked our connection to technology. With the invention of machines like the steam engine, we entered an era of mass production and consumerism.

The emergence of consumerism has been necessary to ensure we purchase goods and cherish capitalism. Have you ever noticed how advertisements get into your head, convincing you that you will be happier with a particular product?

Sometimes, we may even feel like we are not enough, so we buy something to feel better. In the past, people’s wishes and welfare depended on their community and family.

Now it’s the responsibility of the government and the market to fulfill our desires. For example, people used to need their children to provide for them when they grew old; now, the market provides us with jobs, pensions, and insurance.

Historically, communities and families have imposed constraints on our freedom by determining our destiny. Still, as time has progressed, we have gained the ability to make choices regarding our partners and life paths.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Reality splits between tangible nature and shared fictions — governments, corporations, human rights. Stop believing and institutions vanish; forests remain.

Everything around us, from the Internet to governments, is a product of our minds. And our desires are based on society's most famous myths.

Are we happier than ancestors? One view cites neurochemistry: serotonin and dopamine outweigh bank balances. Another finds meaning in purposeful struggle. Buddhism offers a third path — accept feeling without endless craving.

Language and cooperation granted planetary dominance. Using that power responsibly is the unfinished chapter.

Try this

• Notice which social myths shape your choices.
• Scrutinize purchases for environmental and ethical impact.
• Favor companies committed to nonviolence and social responsibility.

Our reality has two dimensions: the one we can see — forests, animals, and lakes — and the “fictions” we invented — governments, companies, and nations. The fate of the first dimension depends on what we imagine (the second dimension) and how we picture it.

All things apart from science and objective reality are myths that enable us to build a prosperous society. As a result, if we stop believing in them, even concepts such as human rights and “Apple” will also disappear.

Do we feel happier than our ancestors in this capitalist-consumerist society? One perspective is that our happiness depends primarily on biochemistry and less on psychological and sociological conditions.
